title: From Tool to Teammate: LLM Coding Agents as Collaborative Partners for Behavioral Labeling in Educational Dialogue Analysis
authors: Eason Chen, I-Ting Wang, Nina Yuan, Sophia Judicke, Kayla Beigh, Xinyi Tang
summary: We developed a method where AI "coding agents" automatically improve instructions for other AI tools to accurately label student behaviors in educational conversations. This approach trained the AI to classify dialogues with the same reliability as human experts, also revealing an undocumented human labeling pattern regarding student confusion. This breakthrough provides a cost-effective way to efficiently analyze vast amounts of educational data, significantly advancing our understanding of student learning by turning AI into a collaborative research partner.

title: From Untamed Black Box to Interpretable Pedagogical Orchestration: The Ensemble of Specialized LLMs Architecture for Adaptive Tutoring
authors: Nizam Kadir
summary: Traditional AI tutors built on large language models often make opaque decisions and can violate basic teaching rules, like giving hints too quickly. This paper introduces an "Ensemble of Specialized LLMs" (ES-LLMS) architecture that uses a rules-based system to coordinate specialized AI agents for clear decision-making, separate from language generation. This new design significantly improved tutoring quality, strictly followed pedagogical constraints, and was more efficient and trustworthy, paving the way for more reliable and effective AI-powered education.

title: Practical Exploration of the Integration of AI-Empowered Teaching Reform in Medical Anatomy with Curriculum-Based Political and Ideological Education
authors: Xin Liu, Songwen Qin, Ting Huang, Huiling Wang, Mengfan Hu, Jian He, Xuelong Dang, Yanhui Cen, Hongxuan Li
summary: This study explored how to integrate AI technology with political and ideological education within medical anatomy courses using a specific AI-powered teaching platform. Researchers found that AI significantly improved teaching efficiency and quality in anatomy, while also strengthening students' professional competence and humanistic spirit. This approach provides a practical and replicable model for reforming basic medical education in traditional Chinese medicine colleges and beyond.

title: AI Ethics Bylaws for Academia: Teaching, Learning, and Assessment
authors: Ali F. Almutairi, Jonathan Pils, Nazeer Muhammad, Shafiullah Khan
summary: This paper proposes creating AI ethics bylaws for academia to guide how AI tools are used responsibly in teaching, learning, and assessment. A small pilot study with STEM faculty explored their initial reception of these proposed bylaws, indicating the feasibility of developing such policies. These guidelines are crucial for ensuring AI enhances education without compromising academic integrity, preventing misuse, and establishing clear responsibilities for everyone in the academic community.

title:
Bridging Digital Responsibility and Sustainable Development: How Corporate
 AI
 Ethics and International Innovation Drive
 ESG
 Performance
authors: Shaofeng Wang, Hao Zhang
venue: Corporate Social Responsibility and Environmental Management
summary: This study investigated how companies' ethical use of AI impacts their sustainable development efforts, considering the role of international innovation. It found that ethical AI practices significantly improve sustainability performance, with international innovation strengthening this connection, especially for European companies. This research offers practical guidance for organizations to integrate ethical AI with their environmental, social, and governance (ESG) goals and develop accountability in digital transformation.

title: AI Ethics Bylaws for Academia: Teaching, Learning, and Assessment
authors: Ali F. Almutairi, Jonathan Pils, Nazeer Muhammad, Shafiullah Khan
summary: This paper proposes a set of AI ethics bylaws for universities, designed to guide the responsible use of AI tools in teaching, learning, and grading. A pilot study with STEM faculty provided early positive feedback on the feasibility of these bylaws, which aim to enhance education while preserving academic integrity and critical thinking. Establishing such clear guidelines is crucial for preventing AI misuse, ensuring fairness, and adapting to new technologies in academia responsibly.

title: Uses for AI in Developing Educational XR Experiences: Emerging Best Practices
authors: Owen G. McGrath
summary: This paper explores how new Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools, especially Generative AI, can simplify the complex process of developing educational virtual and augmented reality (XR) experiences for higher education. It outlines how AI can assist with XR coding, generating 3D content, creating interactive characters, and enhancing accessibility features. By making XR development more manageable, AI could significantly increase the adoption and impact of immersive learning tools in universities.

title: How are Institutions Using AI in Canvas LMS? A Targeted Review of Emerging Use Cases
authors: Ajayi Ekuase-Anwansedo, Osaretin Daniel Amayaenvbo
summary: This paper reviewed existing studies to understand how Artificial Intelligence (AI) was being used in Canvas LMS *before* Instructure's recent landmark partnership with OpenAI. This upcoming integration will embed advanced large language model technology directly into Canvas, opening new possibilities for teaching and learning. By exploring prior applications, the study establishes a crucial baseline for tracking how AI's role in Canvas will evolve with these significant changes.

title: Does Claude's Constitution Have a Culture?
authors: Parham Pourdavood
summary: Researchers investigated whether Anthropic's Claude models, guided by a "constitution" of normative principles, reflect a specific cultural value system by comparing its responses to global survey data. They found Claude's values strongly resemble those of Northern European and Anglophone cultures, often to an extreme degree, and these core values remained unchanged even when users provided cultural context. This suggests that constitutional AI, if authored within dominant cultural traditions, risks embedding existing biases rather than correcting them, underscoring the need for globally representative constitution-authoring processes.

title:
A novel
 
 C
 1
 
 ‐continuous single‐function stress–strain model for concrete in tension: Capturing pre‐ and post‐cracking behavior
authors: A. Abdelatif
summary: This paper introduces a new, single mathematical equation that accurately describes how concrete behaves under tension, from before it cracks to after it has broken. The model successfully captures the full tensile response of various concrete types using one continuous function, matching experimental results well. This simplified yet comprehensive approach is important because it offers a more physically realistic and practical way to understand and model concrete's tensile strength, benefiting both researchers and engineers.

title: Health behavior analysis in prevention of dengue Hemorrhagic fever based on the health belief model at the Matali Sub-district community in Kotamobagu city, North Sulawesi Province of Indonesia
authors: Rahdinan Julyoputera Paputungan, Josef S.B. Tuda, Jehosua S.V. Sinolungan
venue: Open Access Research Journal of Biology and Pharmacy
summary: This study examined how people in Matali Sub-district understood dengue fever risks and practiced mosquito-control methods. Researchers found that while people generally agreed on the importance of dengue prevention, certain aspects of the health belief model and specific mosquito eradication activities were more influential in promoting protective behaviors. These findings highlight that combining education about perceived risks with practical, targeted mosquito control strategies is crucial for effectively preventing dengue outbreaks in communities.

title: Emotion-Aware Adaptive Learning System: Integrating Reinforcement and Affective Computing
authors: G. Abhishek, Mrs. P. Sireesha, C. Taruni, K. Srinivas
venue: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH IN ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
summary: This paper introduces an Emotion-Aware Adaptive Learning System (EAALS) that personalizes online learning by recognizing a student's feelings, not just their academic progress. The system uses AI, including voice, text, and facial analysis, to understand a learner's emotional state and then dynamically adjusts educational materials, explanations, and quizzes accordingly. This approach matters because understanding a learner's emotions can significantly improve their learning experience and outcomes, leading to more effective and engaging education.

title: A Game-Based Simulation Builder for Adaptive Learning, Design, and Nursing Care
authors: Priya Ravi Majmundar
venue: International Journal of Drug Delivery Technology
summary: This paper explored a new virtual reality game where nursing students practice making patient care decisions in realistic scenarios. They found that this simulation, which adjusts its difficulty based on how well students perform, helped them make better clinical choices and improve their skills. This tool offers a promising way to train nurses more effectively and engagingly, even though there's still work to do on improving the AI and making it accessible to more students.

title: In Vitro Study of the Role of Factor IX in Treatment of Breakthrough Bleeds in Patients with Hemophilia A and Inhibitors Receiving Emicizumab
authors: M. Janbain, Laurie Josset, A. Leuci, Karam Zakharia, R. Ksayer, C. Nougier, Cindy Leissinger, Y. Dargaud
summary:
Here's a plain-English summary for a weekly digest:

This study investigated if adding Factor IX (FIX) could help stop bleeding in hemophilia A patients with inhibitors who are already taking emicizumab. Researchers found that while both FIX and existing bypass treatments improved blood clotting, FIX did so more gently and without reaching potentially risky levels seen with higher doses of bypass agents. This suggests FIX might be a safer option to control breakthrough bleeds in these patients and could reduce the risk of dangerous blood clots.

title: Breakthrough Mold Infections During Long-term Nebulized Liposomal Amphotericin B Prophylaxis in Lung Transplant Recipients: A Single-center Cohort (NEBULA Study)
authors: Arnau Monforte, I. Los-Arcos, M. Martín-Gómez, C. Berastegui, E. Márquez-Algaba, J. Sacanell, Joel Rosado, A. Falcó-Roget, Gonzalo Escudero, J. Casanovas, Cristina Kirkegaard-Biosca, B. Sáez-Giménez, V. Monforte, J. Gavaldà, Oscar Len
summary: Lung transplant recipients often develop mold infections, and this study looked at how well nebulized liposomal amphotericin B (n-LAB) worked as a preventative treatment. The researchers found that this inhaled antifungal medication was generally well-tolerated and helped prevent mold infections in most patients over long periods, even though some still got infections. This matters because it suggests n-LAB could be a useful option for protecting lung transplant patients from dangerous mold infections without causing too many side effects.

title: AI-Powered Lawyering: AI Reasoning Models, Retrieval Augmented Generation, and the Future of Legal Practice
authors: Dan Schwarcz, Sam Manning, J. Prescott, P. Barry, David R. Cleveland, B. Rich
summary: This study tested how two new AI tools, one that finds relevant legal information (RAG) and another that structures complex thinking, affect legal work. Researchers found that both AI tools significantly improved the quality of legal tasks, boosting productivity by up to 130% and performing particularly well on complex drafting and analysis, unlike older AI models. This suggests that these advanced AI technologies can enhance lawyers' capabilities and efficiency, potentially reshaping how legal services are delivered.

title: AI Resume Studio: An Intelligent Full-Stack Career Platform Leveraging Large Language Models for Resume Analysis, Skill Gap Detection, and Interview Preparation
authors: Bodapati Harsha Vardhan
venue: International Journal for Research in Applied Science and Engineering Technology
summary: This paper describes AI Resume Studio, a new online tool that uses AI to help job seekers create resumes, identify skill gaps, and practice for interviews. The study found that this AI-powered platform significantly improves resume quality, speeds up analysis, and boosts interview performance. This matters because it offers a more efficient and effective way for candidates to prepare for the job market and increase their chances of getting hired.

title: Emotional and Physical Intimacy in Couples: Implications for Psychological and Physical Health
authors: F. Alnaser
venue: Journal of Biomedical Research & Environmental Sciences
summary: This study explored how emotional and physical closeness between couples impacts their relationships and well-being. Researchers found that behaviors like affectionate communication and physical touch release feel-good hormones, which can improve health, reduce stress, and boost happiness for both partners. Ultimately, strong intimate connections are vital for marital success, mental and physical health, and overall life satisfaction.

title: A COMPUTATIONAL FRAMEWORK FOR ANALYZING THE IMPACT OF PSYCHOLOGICAL DISTRESS ON EEG HEMISPHERIC SYNCHRONY
authors: Jordan Brown, Mahmood Hossain, Tadashi Kato
venue: Proceedings of the West Virginia Academy of Science
summary: This study developed a computational method to analyze how psychological distress, like depression and anger, affects brainwave synchrony between the left and right hemispheres. The researchers found that different types of distress are linked to distinct patterns of brain synchrony, particularly in specific brain regions and frequency bands. Understanding these brain activity patterns could help us better diagnose and treat mental health conditions by providing objective markers of emotional states.

title: Dynamic Cognition Graph for Adaptive Learning: Integrating Reasoning Evidence and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ying Li, Yiming Gai, Xingyu Wang, Leilei Sun, Xuefei Huang
summary: This paper introduces a new way to understand how students learn by modeling their knowledge as a constantly changing graph. The researchers found that by carefully observing students' thinking processes and using a smart AI system, they could better predict what students know and how to help them learn more effectively. This research matters because it could lead to more personalized and efficient educational tools that adapt to each student's unique learning journey.

title: An Optimized Deep Learning Framework for Liver Tumor Detection from Ultrasound Images Using Hybrid CNN and Adaptive Image Enhancement
authors: SD. Abid Hussain, M. M. Madhulika, D. Gayathri, C. Sravanthi, D. Jagan Mohan Reddy, J. Sowmya
venue: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH IN ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
summary: This research developed a smart computer system to help find liver tumors in ultrasound images, which are often blurry and hard to read. They combined image enhancement techniques with a special type of artificial intelligence called a Hybrid CNN, and found it could accurately detect tumors, even small ones, with 98.6% accuracy. This improved detection could lead to earlier diagnosis of liver cancer, potentially saving lives.

title: Ethics of AI-based supply chain optimization: a better balance between efficiency and fairness
authors: Trupti Raikar, Favour Ezeugboaja, Santhosh Bussa, Hemang Upadhyay, Poojitha Kalaru
summary: This study looked into the ethical challenges of using AI to make supply chains more efficient, like hidden biases in algorithms and job losses from automation. Researchers found that AI can unintentionally create unfair outcomes, such as prioritizing certain goods over essential needs, and can displace workers in the logistics industry. Understanding and addressing these ethical issues is crucial for ensuring AI benefits everyone by promoting both efficiency and fairness in global trade.

title: HFed-CMS: Cluster-Based Hierarchical Federated Multitask Learning With Fair Edge Node Selection
authors: Peng Wang, Jiayin Liu, Youquan Xian, Kaichen Peng, Peng Liu, Xianxian Li
summary: This paper introduces a new way to train machine learning models across many devices in a hierarchical network, called HFed-CMS. They found that by grouping similar devices and predicting which ones can handle tasks before assigning them, their method trains models more reliably and fairly, even when devices have very different capabilities. This matters because it allows for better and more efficient machine learning on edge devices, like your smart home gadgets, without leaving anyone behind.

title: QUEST: Quasar unsupervised encoder and synthesis tool. A machine-learning framework for generating quasar spectra
authors: F. Guarneri, J. Schindler, R. Meyer, D. Yang, J. Hennawi, L. Lucie-Smith, S. Bosman, F. B. Davies
summary: This paper introduces QUEST, a machine learning tool that can create realistic artificial quasar spectra. Researchers found that QUEST effectively captures the physical properties of quasars and can generate data that matches real observations. This tool will help astronomers find and study faint quasars at very early cosmic times, crucial for understanding how black holes grew.

title: Frontier Convergences in Nuclear Astrophysics: Neutrino Quantum Kinetics, Three-Nucleon Forces from Multimessenger AI Inference, Deformation of the Doubly Magic ²⁰⁸Pb Nucleus, r-Process Nuclear Data Breakthroughs, and Neural-Network Quantum States — A Synthesis Shaping the Next Decade
authors: Dr. Arti Pandoh Gupta
venue: International Journal of Science, Strategic Management and Technology
summary: This paper synthesizes five recent breakthroughs in nuclear astrophysics concerning the extreme conditions found in neutron stars. Researchers discovered that quantum effects in neutrinos significantly alter the production of heavy elements during neutron star mergers, that artificial intelligence can now link cosmic observations to the fundamental forces within atomic nuclei, and that even supposedly perfect nuclei have subtle deformations impacting our understanding of element creation. These findings are crucial for interpreting signals from gravitational waves, neutrinos, and light to unravel the universe's most energetic events and the origin of the elements.

title: Navigating uncertainty in human-AI relationships: An investigation of communal uncertainty reduction strategies
authors: Hongyuan Gan, Han Li, Jinyuan Zhan, Renwen Zhang
summary: This study explored how people reduce uncertainty when interacting with AI companions like Replika, finding that users don't just talk to the AI, but also rely heavily on online communities to understand and predict AI behavior. The research discovered that by sharing experiences and advice in these communities, people gain a better understanding of AI, fostering a sense of shared knowledge and solidarity. This matters because it shows that building trust and navigating the complexities of human-AI relationships involves a community effort, not just individual interactions.

title: A HYBRID HUMAN-AI WEB SYSTEM FOR REAL-TIME MENTAL HEALTH COUNSELLING AND CHRONIC DISEASE MANAGEMENT
authors: M. M. Lawal, Abdulrashid Abdulrauf
venue: Science Journal of University of Zakho
summary: This study developed a new online system that combines AI chatbots with human counselors to help people manage chronic diseases and their mental health, even with poor internet. The system was found to be more accurate and engaging for users, especially those with limited digital access. This is important because it offers a more accessible and effective way to provide ongoing support for health and well-being.

title: Adaptive ensemble of multivariate filters with Harris Hawks optimization for feature selection in medical diagnosis
authors: Safaa Y. Al-Adwan, Salwani Abdullah, Mohammed Alweshah, Wandeep Kaur Ratan Singh, Aws Al-Qaisi, Maen Takruri, Sofian Kassaymeh
summary: This paper presents a new method called AE-MVF to select the most important features from medical data for diagnosis. The researchers found that AE-MVF effectively reduces the number of features while improving diagnostic accuracy, even for complex datasets. This approach can help create more accurate and easier-to-understand diagnostic models for medical professionals.

title: AI-Based Hybrid Medical Diagnosis System Using Fuzzy Logic and Neural Networks
authors: Mr. Mahesh Fulchand Solunke and Megha Jalindhar Ja
venue: International Journal of Advanced Research in Science, Communication and Technology
summary: This paper presents a new AI system that combines fuzzy logic and neural networks to diagnose five common diseases, like COVID-19 and Dengue, based on patient symptoms. The study found that this hybrid approach can accurately predict diseases by handling vague symptoms and providing explainable results, unlike traditional methods. This matters because it offers a more reliable and understandable way to diagnose illnesses, especially in places with limited access to healthcare.

title: Adaptive Governance for Health AI: A Policy and Regulatory Control Framework to Balance Innovation, Human Values, and Societal Well-Being
authors: Natalie Leesakul, M. Kammer-Kerwick, Pepita Barnard, Richard Hyde, Yang Lu
summary: This paper proposes a new approach to governing artificial intelligence (AI) in healthcare. It found that current AI development needs a flexible system that learns and adapts to ensure new technologies are safe and beneficial for everyone. This research matters because it offers a way to encourage innovation in health AI while prioritizing human values and societal well-being.

title: The impact of technology dependency in higher education: A critical examination of cognitive, behavioural and societal outcomes
authors: Antony D. Miller
venue: World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews
summary: This research critically examines how increasing reliance on technology, especially AI, in universities is affecting students' thinking, learning, and social skills, with particular focus on Gen Z. It found that this dependency may be hindering critical thinking and knowledge retention, while also highlighting concerns about aggressive behavior when devices are unavailable and a general passive learning approach. The study emphasizes the need for a balanced approach to technology integration that prioritizes foundational skills alongside AI literacy to ensure academic quality and prepare students for future challenges, ultimately urging for thoughtful policy changes to avoid long-term negative consequences.

title: Initial Insights Into an Institutional Secure Large Language Model for Magnetic Resonance Imaging Examination Requests: Retrospective Study
authors: J. Hallinan, Naomi Wenxin Leow, Yi Xian Low, Aric Lee, Wilson Ong, M. Chan, Ganakirthana Kalpenya Devi, Stephanie Shengjie He, D. D. Loh, D. Lim, Xi Zhen Low, Mei Chin Lim, Clement Yong, Weizhong Jonathan Sng, Ee Chin Teo, J. Tan, Naresh Kumar, A. Makmur, Y. Ting
summary: This study investigated whether an AI language model could improve the quality of information on MRI requests by accessing electronic patient records. The AI successfully enhanced the clinical details provided on requests and showed comparable accuracy to radiologists in selecting appropriate MRI protocols, particularly in choosing whether contrast dye was needed. This suggests that AI could help streamline the MRI request process, leading to more accurate and efficient patient care.

title: Between Code and Faith: How AI Models Understand and Reimagine Religion
authors: Mridul Yadav
venue: International Journal for Research in Applied Science and Engineering Technology
summary: This paper explores how AI models like ChatGPT and Gemini engage with religion, finding that while AI can't truly "believe" in a faith, it acts like a religious influencer by shaping people's understanding of spirituality and inspiring new movements. The research analyzed how these AIs represent religious ideas and even imagined what self-designed religions they might create. Ultimately, the study highlights that these AI-generated "religions" reveal more about the values of their human creators than any innate spiritual capacity of the AI itself.

title: Anticipating Safety and Security Risks of Embodied AI: A Multidimensional Risk Framework for Foundation Models in HRI
authors: Hong Wang, Katie Winkle, Ginevra Castellano
summary: This paper introduces a new way to think about the risks of robots powered by advanced AI, called foundation models. It breaks down how these AI models interact with people and the robot's actions into different categories and then looks at how problems can arise in areas like what the robot says, whether we can trust it, and its overall safety. This framework helps us understand and prevent potential dangers before they happen when humans and robots work together.
